comment_content:
This isn’t tactics, it’s self-sabotage. We all know Pep’s track record in the UCL. Let me be clear: with those three defenders, there’s absolutely no additional offensive option — their only role is to sit back and protect the zone. That means any opposing player can literally stroll up to the halfway line, grab a coffee, and just wait for a long ball (ask Fernando Torres about that ) Honestly, Pep is one of the worst tactical managers I’ve seen in 30 years of watching football. 

And let’s be honest: Thierry Henry is one of the most clueless analysts out there — he genuinely makes me laugh. I’d advise him to cherish his Arsenal years, because that was clearly the best period of his career. You’d think he’d feel a bit embarrassed about his time under Pep at Barcelona. His role was basically to stay far away from the play, just stretching the pitch to give Messi and Iniesta more space — he was practically glued to the touchline. It’s like he completely forgot the moment he stepped inside, linked up with Messi and Iniesta, scored, and Pep wasn’t even happy because Henry didn’t stick to “enlarging the space” as instructed. The whole thing is honestly absurd.

Pep: footbal plays on 3 dimensions not 2D

comment_content:
I always stayed behind the defenders. Even in their own half. It was driving them crazy. Always switching between the center backs, always offside.

I knew my teammates and when they would pass me the ball, so I just dipped back to be onside and then just run. Was so effective. And it doesn‘t really matter which league. They constantly need to keep an eye on you, communicate etc. No break for them.

The cool thing is that if they decide to stay behind you, your whole team gained space – and you can go offside again.
The drawback is that some refs/assistants are too dumb to get it.
